我们每天都在互联网的海洋中游泳。我们使用Google Drive，这是生产，我们点餐，我们排泄我们的检疫焦虑，我们发推文，我们创造互联网，我们是由它制造的。
但大多数时候，我们并不考虑互联网实际上是如何运作的。就像在两条鱼的故事中，一条鱼说“这水很脏”，另一条鱼说“水是什么”，我们通常对支撑我们数字生活的基础设施视而不见。我已经写过关于云是如何在犹他州沙漠的某个地方真的是一架又一架的计算机的。我还写过美国和俄罗斯的互联网是如何开始的。
但是这些计算机是如何相互连接的呢？当他们停止连接时会发生什么呢？
很有可能，你是在家里看这份时事通讯的(如果你不在家，我希望你在某个令人惊叹的地方。请为我代为生活。)。在您的家中，有一台由电信提供商(ISP)发送给您的路由器。当您键入vicki.substack.com时，您的计算机会将该信息发送到您的路由器。如果您的路由器将其发送到电信公司运营的(技术术语)Big Router。这就是所谓的过境。然后，该路由器将其传递给另一台路由器，再由另一台路由器将其传递给另一台路由器，直到它到达正确的位置。
从本质上讲，互联网是一组相互交谈的庞大而松散的计算机集合。他们是如何相互交谈的呢？他们发送数据包。数据包本质上是信封，上面有它们来自的计算机的地址和它们将要到达的地址。信封内是一封信，由1和0组成，它们要么构成一条完整的信息，要么构成一大段信息的一部分，这些信息是根据其他也有数据包的信封组合在一起的。
如果您想了解有关数据包的更多信息，我强烈建议您研究一下Wireshark。(顺便说一句，如果您对数据包的外观感兴趣，那么当您加载网站时，它们大概是HTTP POST/GET请求，我们在前面讨论Nginx时谈到了这一点。)。
通常，各国都有多个ISP来协调和传递彼此之间以及其他国家的ISP之间的流量。这就是所谓的窥视。
对等协议-互联网的价值创造引擎-是运营商互连协议，允许运营商交换发往另一个客户的流量；它们在互联网的核心最常见，其中拓扑由密集互连的网络组成，这些网络主要与代表其客户的网络的流量传输有关。
在全部分析的协议中，1347份(0.07%)是以书面合同形式正式确定的。这低于2011年的0.49%。其余1,934,166宗(99.93%)属“握手”协议，双方同意非正式或一般理解的用语，而无须订立书面文件。
考虑到所有这些复杂程度和国际政治，互联网的运作实际上是相当令人惊讶的。所有这些合作提供互联网的非正式协议是我没有完全认输的原因之一。
然而，有些情况下会出现令人难以置信的错误，这正是几周前在白俄罗斯发生的情况。(根据一些说法，截至本周，这种情况仍在发生。)。
白俄罗斯在苏联解体后获得独立，但却由亚历山大·卢卡申科(Alexander Lukashenko)领导，卢卡申科是一位前苏联强人，在过去的25年里一直担任“总统”。(不出所料，他也是白俄罗斯政府中唯一在最高委员会投票反对苏联决议的人。)。20世纪90年代初，卢卡申科巩固了自己的权力，到目前为止，这个国家或多或少一直是一个独裁国家。(哦，还有一个好消息，白俄罗斯没有令人满意的东西。)。
考虑到过去30年来白俄罗斯在政治上的瘫痪，白俄罗斯在建立一个强大的网络网络以连接外部互联网方面存在问题。即使到了2010年，人们仍然在使用自制的局域网，字面意思是连接在公寓楼之间的计算机。
在白俄罗斯，住宅互联网接入仍然明显负担不起，主要是因为BelTelecom继续保持国家垄断。白俄罗斯国家提供商直到1999年才为住宅互联网接入推出廉价的拨号服务。私营互联网服务提供商也被要求只能以高价从垄断提供商那里购买互联网流量，从而限制互联网服务提供商只能将服务转售给最终用户。私营ISP专注于向企业和其他组织出售互联网接入，因为他们的服务对个人家庭互联网用户来说太贵了。
在这段时间里，白俄罗斯基本上只有一家ISP，即BelTelecom。所有的互联网流量，所有的数据包，进出这个国家都必须通过BelTelecom，即使你是一家规模较小的ISP。你可以将对等权转售给其他较小的ISP，你也可以路由流量，但最终，如果你想与Facebook、谷歌或任何服务器位于白俄罗斯以外的网站交谈，你将会遇到Big Bel。这可能就是谷歌几年前讨好他们的原因。
8月9日，白俄罗斯举行了选举。考虑到你现在对白俄罗斯的所有了解，你可能会同意，选举这个词在这里做了很多工作。然而，这一次出现了强大的反对派，卢卡申科当然被监禁了。当他这样做时，对手的妻子，一名英语教师和两个年幼孩子的母亲，取代了他的位置，获得了巨大的人气和群众支持，以至于开始动摇独裁统治的幻想。
在选举之前，关于互联网带宽被限制的报道开始通过俄语新闻网站出现。
关于白俄罗斯可能在总统竞选期间禁用互联网接入的报道在总统选举结束前几天首次出现。例如，8月4日，反对党电报频道Nexta Live发布了一封信的图片，其中当地一家公司的经理通知员工互联网关闭的可能性，并列出了为此类活动做准备的说明。主要媒体很快开始报道类似的信息，援引匿名消息人士的话说，停电将于8月8日晚些时候(投票结束前一天)开始。
一些商店开始张贴用信用卡付款已不可能的标志。俄语科技媒体开始猜测DDOS攻击。
第一批关于“Bynet”中断的报道出现在8月8日晚些时候。第二天的停电情况更加严重，大量白俄罗斯互联网用户表示，他们无法访问YouTube、社交网络Twitter、Facebook和Instagram，以及即时通讯工具WhatsApp、Telegram和Viber，也无法访问谷歌、Yandex和Mail.Ru等各种搜索引擎。许多人还报告了在线支付系统的问题。Wi-Fi和移动互联网信号都失去了与万维网的连接。在白俄罗斯境外，互联网用户在访问属于白俄罗斯的.by域名上的网站时开始出现问题。
那天晚上，在白俄罗斯，联系任何人的唯一途径就是手机。正如人权观察的一名编辑所报道的那样，
周一，当我越过边境进入白俄罗斯时，我的互联网断了。我当时正前往明斯克报道政府对上周末有争议的选举后史无前例的公众动员的反应。
“那么，选举结果怎么样了？”当我从俄罗斯越境进入白俄罗斯时，迎接我的出租车司机问道。由于没有互联网，他不知道总统声称他赢得了80%的选票。在明斯克，十几辆满载武装人员的卡车驶过。在我住的公寓里，我终于可以接入Wi-Fi了。这个问题比移动互联网被切断更重要。如果不使用额外的工具绕过审查，所有常见的消息服务都不能工作，所以对于白俄罗斯的大多数人来说，目前没有安全的通信是可能的。
目前还不完全清楚发生了什么，但鉴于我们对数据包和网络的了解，我们可以推测。BelTelecom的大路由器就像俱乐部的保镖。(对不起，我不能把夜总会从脑海中抹去。)。所有的信息包就像人们进出俱乐部一样。一个人走到保镖跟前说，我想去贵宾区(欧盟)，让我进去。保镖检查了他们，看了看他的名单，然后说，好的，你可以进来了。另一个人走过来说，我是EDM的超级粉丝，我需要坐在DJ旁边声音最大的地方，保镖点点头，把他们带到DJ摊位旁边的地方(俄罗斯)。其他人说，我只想去酒吧，保镖让他们去酒吧(美国)。如果俱乐部管理层觉得去美国的人太多，可以给保镖一份名单，保镖说，“对不起，酒吧客满了。你不能进来，“人/包必须呆在外面。
然后，人们也会离开俱乐部。如果经理因为想让人们看起来客满而不想让他们离开俱乐部，它可以告诉保镖通过给贵宾提供饮料来阻止他们离开。
8月9日，白俄罗斯政府给了这名保镖一份名单，上面写着，任何人都不能离开或进入俱乐部。所有数据必须保持原样。由于BelTelecom控制着全国所有的交通出入口，保镖可以很容易地按照方向办事。而且它还切断了互联网。车流量降到了涓涓细流。
如何绕过控制整个国家的保镖/恶霸？人们立即开始寻找工作场所。
他们转向两个主要的地方。第一个是加拿大的一款名为Psiphon的应用程序，它允许你通过组合使用VPN、SSH和代理等工具来假装你不在你所在的国家，所有这些工具的工作原理都相似，它们将你连接到房子外的一台电脑上，希望你能连接到这个国家，并假装你从那里发送所有的数据包，而不是从你的家庭电脑发送。
由于你需要通过应用商店或者通过下载并(在任何应用商店之外)将其侧面加载到你的设备上来安装这款应用，所以人们会提供随附的闪存驱动器，并将其传递给其他人。
似乎白俄罗斯的每个人都在关注Nexta Live。在该国总统选举后的头几天，当整个国家几乎完全离线时，电报信使服务上的这个频道是关于不断升级的政治危机的仅有的信息来源之一。
我以前写过我有多喜欢电报，以及开放交流是多么重要。
2018年，政府试图阻止访问由帕维尔·杜罗夫(Pavel Durov)运营的俄罗斯最受欢迎的通讯平台Telegram。他之前创建了VKontakte，并与希望他撤下反对派候选人页面的政客们发生了麻烦。他拒绝这样做。2014年，他拒绝交出乌克兰抗议者的数据，并拒绝屏蔽俄罗斯反对派名义领袖阿列克谢·纳瓦尔尼(Alexei Navalny)的页面。结果，他被罢免了公司首席执行官的职务，并离开了俄罗斯。他目前是圣基茨和尼维斯的公民，他通过“向该国的糖业多元化基金会捐赠25万美元，并在瑞士银行获得3亿美元现金”获得了这一公民身份。顺便说一句，所有这些都只是维基百科的表层内容。
在这一切之后，他创立了Telegram，这是一家总部位于柏林的即时通讯服务，其工作原理与Whatsapp非常相似，不同之处在于它不属于Facebook，不会解密你的消息。
Telegram在俄罗斯变得非常流行，无论是因为私人聊天，还是因为订阅了充满新闻、笑话和更多的频道。顺便说一句，Telegram是我个人最喜欢的消息服务。它快速，简单，效果很好，而且还因为它真的做得很好。
贴纸的概念很好--你还能在哪里找到一包Mark Lizardberg的贴纸呢？
这是白俄罗斯仅有的继续半工作的服务之一，从表面上看，这也是组织大规模和平抗议的抗议者组织的方式之一。
在互联网关闭几天后，它断断续续，一开始是薄片状的，后来完全打开了。为什么？正如萨沙在他的俄语子栈(通常是关于广告)中所写的那样，政府真的不想关闭互联网。为什么？因为政府也依赖它，整个经济，即使在白俄罗斯这样的独裁国家，仍然依赖于现代互联网。
当然，也有社交媒体网站，但让我们考虑一下其他依赖互联网的东西：信用卡交易、银行通信、医院、政府官员之间的短信、电子邮件、一些电网的某些部分、(非常可怕和脆弱的)物联网、杂货店，等等，直到并包括试图阻止它的政府。如果他们不在同一个房间，他们将如何协调？建造一个掩体？
政府不能长时间关闭互联网，因为政府和我们这些凡人一样，也需要互联网。互联网，就像现在已经很成熟的那样，是一种公用事业，与水、电、热一样重要。如果你关闭互联网，你不仅会发生大规模兵变，还会面临经济危机和数十万人的死亡。
正如一位人士所说，在6月份的一次非常好的采访(俄罗斯)中，实际上预测到了这一点，
«Из-заотключенияинтернетаполучаетсяещебольшийскандал，чемеслибыкого-тоизбили»又名，“与他们(政府)殴打和折磨人民相比，关闭互联网会引起更大的争议。”
最后，尽你所能的努力，你无论如何都不能关闭整个互联网，因为还有其他电信专家所说的进出白俄罗斯的“海盗线”。例如，俄罗斯石油公司Transneft基本上拥有自己的遍布全国的互联网。
截至本周，互联网大部分时间都在试探性地卷土重来。我们将拭目以待白俄罗斯的未来，但我认为这在几个方面非常能说明问题。首先，互联网对每个人的效用是大多数地方自身保护的保障。其次，事实是，归根结底，互联网是一个奇迹，是人类成就的巅峰，但让它团结和运转的是人，自愿同意对等和运输协议的人，要求和需要它的人，以及冒着一切风险愿意提供它的人。第三，考虑到帕维尔·杜罗夫(Pavel Durov)和现在的Psiphon与Facebook相比的影响力，他在西方媒体上的报道还不够充分。有人会说这会产生反作用的影响。
我非常非常希望白俄罗斯实现全面过渡，人民再次掌握国家命运。现在是2020年，所以我尽量不要求太多，但我希望这一年能给我们带来这一点，免费的互联网将会获胜。
更多关于我几周前关于谷歌是如何成为互联网的帖子。
这份时事通讯的方式是接受植根于人文主义、细微差别、背景、理性和一点乐趣的科技新闻。它一周出去一到两次。如果你喜欢，就把它转发给朋友，告诉他们订阅吧！
作者：我是一名机器学习工程师。我的大部分空闲时间都花在了为学龄前儿童和婴儿争吵、阅读和写糟糕的推文上。点击这里了解更多信息或在推特上关注我。
note_en:
We swim in the seas of the internet every single day.  We use  Google Drive, which is production, we order  food, we channel our  quarantine anxieties, we  tweet, we make the internet, and we are made by it.
 But most of the time we don’t think about how the internet actually works. Just like in the story of the two fish where one says “this water’s dirty,” and the other says, “What is water,” we are usually mostly oblivious to the infrastructures supporting our digital lives. I’ve already written about how  the cloud is just really racks and  racks of computers somewhere in the Utah desert.  I’ve also written about how the American and Russian internet  got started.
 But how do those computers connect to each other? And what happens when they stop connecting?
  Chances are, you’re reading this newsletter at home (and if you’re not, I hope you are somewhere amazing. Live vicariously for me, please.) In your home, there is a router, sent to you by a telecom provider (ISP). When you type in vicki.substack.com, your computer sends that information to your router.   Your router sends it to the (technical term) Big Router run by the telecom company. This is known as  transit. That router then passes it on to another router, which passes it to another one, until it gets to the correct place.
 The internet, essentially, is a huge, loose collection of groups of computers talking to each other. How do they talk to each other? They send packets. Packets are essentially envelopes that have the address of the computer they came from and the address they’re going to. Inside the envelope is a letter, made of ones and zeros that form either an entire piece of information, or part of a large piece of information that gets put together based on the other envelopes that also have packets inside them.
 If you want to learn more about packets, I strongly recommend looking into  Wireshark. (By the way, if you’re interested in what packets look like, when you load websites, they’re approximately  HTTP POST/GET requests,  which we covered before when we talked about Nginx.)
   Usually, countries have  a number of ISPs that coordinate and pass on traffic between themselves, and between ISPs in other countries. This is known as  peering.
 Peering agreements – the value-creation engine of the Internet – are the carrier interconnection agreements that allow carriers to exchange traffic bound for one another’s customers; they are most common in the core of the Internet, where the topology consists of densely interconnected networks that are principally concerned with the carriage of traffic on behalf of the networks which are their customers.
 Of the total analyzed agreements, 1,347 (0.07%) were formalized in written contracts. This is down from 0.49% in 2011. The remaining 1,934,166 (99.93%) were “handshake” agreements in which the parties agreed to informal or commonly understood terms without creating a written document.
 Given all these levels of complexity and international politics, it’s actually pretty amazing the internet works at all. And all these informal agreements working together to provide the internet is one of the reasons I haven’t completely thrown the towel in on humanity.
 However, there are cases when this goes incredibly wrong, and that’s exactly happened in Belarus several weeks ago. (And is still happening as of this week by some accounts.)
  Belarus is became independent when the Soviet Union fell, only to be led by Alexander Lukashenko, an ex-Soviet strongman who’s been the “president” for the last 25 years. (Unsurprisingly, he was also the only dude in government in Belarus at the Supreme Council to vote against the resolution of the Soviet Union.) In the early 1990s, Lukashenko consolidated his power, and, up to now the country has more-or-less been a dictatorship. (Oh, and also, great news,  there’s no covid in Belarus.)
 Given how it’s been crippled politically over the past 30 years, Belarus has had problems establishing a robust network of networks to connect to the outside internet. Even as late as 2010, people were still using homebrewed LAN networks, literally computers  connected between apartment buildings.
 In Belarus, residential Internet access remained significantly less affordable, primarily due to the continuation of a state monopoly by Beltelecom. The Belarusian state provider did not launch an inexpensive dial-up service for residential Internet-access until 1999. Private ISPs were also required to buy their Internet traffic only from the monopoly provider and at high prices, thus restricting ISPs to re-selling services only to end-users. The private ISPs focused on selling Internet access to businesses and other organizations as their services were too expensive for individual household Internet users.
  For all of this time, Belarus has had, essentially, one ISP, Beltelecom. All internet traffic, all of the packets, coming in and out of the country must go through Beltelecom, even if you are a smaller ISP. You can resell peering rights to other smaller ISPs, and you can route traffic, but ultimately, if you want to talk to Facebook, Google, or literally any site whose servers sit outside of Belarus, you’re gonna encounter Big Bel. Which is probably why  Google cozied up to them a few years ago.
  On August 9, Belarus held elections.  Given everything you now know about Belarus, you’ll probably agree that the word elections is doing a lot of work here. This time, though, there was a robust opposition, which Lukashenko of course jailed. When he did, the opponent’s wife, an English teacher and mom of two small kids, took his place, gaining enormous popularity and mass support, to the point where it started to destabilize the illusion of the dictatorship.
 Before the elections, reports of throttled internet bandwidth started to come through  Russian language news sites.
 Reports that Belarus might disable Internet access during the presidential race first started appearing several days before the presidential election concluded. On August 4, for example, the opposition Telegram channel  Nexta Live  published an image of a letter where a manager at a local company informed his staff about the likelihood of an Internet shutdown and listed instructions to prepare for such an event. Major media outlets soon started  reporting similar information, citing anonymous sources claiming that the blackouts would begin late on August 8 (a day before the end of voting).
 Some stores started posting signs that paying by credit card had become impossible. The Russian-language tech media started speculating about  DDOS attacks.
    The first reports about “Bynet” outages appeared late on August 8. Blackouts were even worse the next day, when large numbers of Belarusian Internet users said they could not access YouTube, the social networks Twitter, Facebook, and Instagram, the instant messengers WhatsApp, Telegram, and Viber, or various search engines like Google, Yandex, and Mail.Ru. Many people also reported problems with  online payment systems. Both Wi-Fi and mobile Internet signals lost their connection to the World Wide Web.Outside Belarus, Internet users started having problems accessing websites hosted on the .by domain, which belongs to Belarus.
 That night, the only way to reach anyone in Belarus was by cell phone. As  a Human Rights Watch editor reported,
 My internet went out when I crossed the border into Belarus on Monday. I was on my way to Minsk to report on the government response to the  unprecedented public mobilization following last weekend’s disputed election.
 “So, what’s up with the election results?” asked the taxi driver who met me as I crossed the border into Belarus from Russia. With no internet, he had no idea the president claimed he won 80 percent of the vote. In Minsk, a dozen trucks full of armed personnel drive past. At the apartment where I stay, I am finally able to get onto Wi-Fi. The issue is more significant than mobile internet being cut off. None of  the usual messaging services work without using additional tools to bypass the censorship, so for most in Belarus no secure communications are currently possible.
  It’s unclear entirely what happened, but, given our knowledge of packets and networks, we can speculate. The Big Router at Beltelecom is like a bouncer at a club. (Sorry, can’t get nightclubs  off my mind.) and all the packets of information are like people entering and leaving a club. A person comes up to the bouncer and says, I want to go to the VIP area (the EU), let me in. The bouncer checks them out, looks at his list, and says, ok you can come in. Another person comes and says, I’m an extreme EDM fan and I need to be in the loudest place next to the DJ, the bouncer nods, and directs them to the space next to the DJ booth (Russia.) Someone else says, I just want to go to the bar, and the bouncer lets them through to the bar (America.)  If the club management is feeling like too many people are going to America, it can give the bouncer a list and the bouncer says, “Sorry, the bar is full. You can’t come in,” and the people/packets have to stay outside.
 And then, people leave the club, too.  If the manager doesn’t want people to leave the club because it wants them to look full, it can tell the bouncer to discourage VIP people from leaving by offering them drinks.
 On August 9, the Government of Belarus gave the bouncer a list that said that no one can either leave or come into the club. All data must stay where it is. Since Beltelecom controls all the ingress/egress of traffic in the country, the bouncer could follow the directions very easily.  And it turned off the internet. Traffic fell to a trickle.
   How do you bypass a bouncer/bully that controls the entire country? Immediately people started looking for work-arounds.
  They turned to two main places. The first was a Canadian-based app called  Psiphon, which allows you to pretend you’re not in the country where you are by using a c ombination of tools like VPNs, SSH, and proxies, all of which work similarly in that they connect you to a computer outside of your house, and hopefully country, and pretend that you’re sending all your packets from there instead of your home computer.
 Since you need to install the app through either the app store or by downloading it and sideloading it (outside of any app store) on your device, people were offering flash drives with it and passing them around.
  It seems that everybody in Belarus follows Nexta Live. During the first days after the country's presidential election, when the entire country went nearly completely offline, this channel on the Telegram messenger service was one of the only sources of information about an escalating political crisis.
 I’ve written before about how much I love Telegram and  how important it is to open communication.
 In 2018, the government tried to block access to Telegram, Russia’s favorite messaging platform, run by Pavel Durov. He had previously created VKontakte, and got in trouble with politicians who wanted him to take down pages of opposition candidates. He declined to do so. In 2014, he refused to hand over data of Ukranian protesters, and refused to block the page of Alexei Navalny, the figurehead of the Russian opposition. As a result, he was ousted as the CEO of the company, and left Russia. He is  currently a citizen of Saint Kitts and Nevis, a citizenship which he obtained through “donating $250,000 to the country's Sugar Industry Diversification Foundation, and secured US$300 million in cash within Swiss banks.” All of this is just the surface-level stuff from Wikipedia, by the way.
 After all of THAT, he founded Telegram, a messaging service based in Berlin which works very much like Whatsapp, except it’s not owned by Facebook and  does not decrypt your messages.
 Telegram became extremely popular in Russia, both for private chats and for subscribing to channels full of news, jokes, and much more. As an aside, Telegram is my personal favorite messaging service. It’s fast, easy, works well, and also because it’s really done
 the concept of stickers well - where else can you find a pack of  Mark Lizardberg stickers?
 And it was one of the only services that continued to semi-work in Belarus, and ostensibly, one of the ways that the protesters who  organized a mass peaceful protest organized.
   Several days after the internet was shut down, it was on and off, flaky at first, and then entirely on. Why?  As Sasha writes in his Russian-language Substack, which is usually about advertising, the  government really didn’t want to shut down the internet. Why? Because the government also relies on it.  The entire economy, even in a dictatorship such as Belarus, still relies on the modern internet.
 Sure, there are social media sites, but let’s think through what else relies on the internet: credit card transactions, bank communications, hospitals, SMS between government officials, email, some parts of some electrical grids, the (very scary and flaky) internet of things, grocery stores, and on and on and on, up to and including the very government that is trying to stop it. How will they coordinate if they’re not in the same room together? Build a bunker?
 The government can’t turn off the internet for long because the government, just like the rest of us mere mortals, also needs the internet. The internet, as has now been well-established, is a public utility, just as important as water, as electricity, as heat. If you shut down the internet, you will not only have a mass mutiny, but an economic crisis and hundreds of thousands of deaths on your hands.
 As one person put it, in a very good interview (Russian) from June  that actually predicted this,
 «Из-за отключения интернета получается еще больший скандал, чем если бы кого-то избили» Aka, “There’s a bigger controversy that arises from turning off the internet, than if they (the government) had beaten and tortured people.”
 And, ultimately, try as hard as you can, you can’t turn off the entire internet anyway because there are other,  what telecom experts have called “pirate lines” in and out of Belarus. For example, Transneft, a Russian oil company, essentially has  its own internet that goes through the country.
   As of this week, the internet is, mostly and tentatively back. We’ll see what the future has in store for Belarus, but I think this has been very telling in a couple of ways.  First, the internet’s utility for everyone is a safeguard for its own protection in most places.  Second, the fact that, when it comes down to it, the internet is a miracle, a pinnacle of human accomplishment, but what keeps it together and running is people, people who voluntarily agree to peering and transit agreements, people who demand it and need it, and people who, risking everything else, are willing to provide it. And third, that Pavel Durov, and now, Psiphon, is not as nearly covered as he should be in the Western media given how much impact he has compared to Facebook. Some would say the counter-acting impact.
 I am very, very hopeful that Belarus makes a full transition, where the people are in control of the destiny of the country once again. It’s 2020, so I’m trying not to ask for too much, but I am hopeful that maybe the year can grace us with this one thing, and the free internet will win.
  More on my post from a few weeks back on  how Google is now the internet
    This newsletter’s M.O. is takes on tech news that are rooted in humanism, nuance, context, rationality, and a little fun. It goes out once or twice a week. If you like it, forward it to friends and tell them to subscribe!
  The Author:  I’m a machine learning engineer. Most of my free time is spent wrangling a preschooler and a baby, reading, and  writing bad tweets. Find out more  here or follow me  on Twitter.

title: 当互联网在白俄罗斯停止时
title_en: When the Internet Stopped in Belarus

奥克塔维亚·巴特勒(Octavia Butler)是一位黑人科幻作家，在N.K.杰米辛(N.K.Jemisin)到来之前，所有其他人都被拿来与之比较。1993年，她出版了小说“播种者的寓言”，这是三部曲的第一部。在她虚构的不久的将来，南加州将成为被指定为地狱角落口袋的新红线地区之一，因为末日即将到来。我在说，海豚在跟我们聊天，“我们出去了！气候、环境和社会崩溃的NO-THANKS-FOR-THE-MERCURY-MARINATED-FISH！”-type。不需要怪兽。在50多年的狂欢之后，我们将死于消费，沉醉于新自由主义的晚期资本主义的兼职。
[[这篇文章发表在“新模式”第一期上。在此购买您的副本或订阅。]]。
快进到我们现在的非虚构类节目。我白天的工作是教小学。然而，尽管有两个月“随心所欲”的诱惑，我越来越害怕夏天的到来，因为岛屿大小的冰川正在落入海洋。这是我的西部世界王国遭受干旱和饥荒侵袭的季节吗？当俄罗斯的永久冻土融化，释放出原始的微观疾病巨人(他们是真实的，2017年)，在一场出人意料的希腊悲剧中吞噬我？当我由亚马逊激发的欲望的闸门被自然的人为灾难永久堵塞时？当然，Prime将继续交付，但可以理解的是，标记将是淫秽的。
我开玩笑是因为我害怕。这种特定的反乌托邦不仅是可能的，巴特勒似乎在27年前就预测到了。播种者的寓言发生在本世纪20年代的一次总统选举中，在那次选举中，候选人的竞选口号是“让美国再次伟大”。哦，是的，而且加州现在实际上正在燃烧。这是个可怕的礼物。我点的和这场噩梦正好相反。我要的是太阳朋克。
太阳朋克是一种具有环保意识的投机艺术运动，最能驾驭未来的恐怖。批评者称其为一种充满空卡路里的盲目乌托邦。诚然，谷歌的复制者机器将提供一场视觉盛宴，展示森林覆盖的摩天大楼和郁郁葱葱的太阳能蘑菇城市(可能在海底，可能与和蔼可亲的、有降落牙的无脊椎动物分享，时间不变，喜剧性十足)。太阳朋克美学有时会穿乌托邦的衣服，但这不是任何人的错，真的。几十年前，乌苏拉·K·勒·奎因(Ursula K.Le Guin)帮助播种了太阳朋克的种子，这些故事包括1972年的小说“被剥夺的人”(The Disposed)，她1982年的散文“加州作为一个寒冷的地方的非欧几里得观点”，以及其他许多受道家启发的作品。因此，早在2008年这个词被创造出来之前，这场运动就已经发展起来了。因此，经过几十年的想象和社区建设，肯定会有层次感和易碎的边缘。公平地说，梦到地狱而不渴望它的对立面是不可能的。
事实上，太阳朋克是功能性AF。它的光环的原色是红色、橙色和黄色：勇敢的同情心、创造性的科学和觉醒的相互依赖。在很多方面，它可以比所谓的“硬科幻小说”更严谨。“不要”信使“；我引用了”纽约2140“的作者金·斯坦利·罗宾逊(Kim Stanley Robinson)的话，以及其他许多开创性的生态小说故事。他对“-朋克”(谷歌朋克流派，看看你能得到多少)的病毒式过度词缀提出异议。然而，在这个国家最大的科幻小说大会之一的Boskone 57上，罗宾逊是主宾，他描述了一种充满未来的体裁，挑战人类世。故事中我们做出了牺牲，但最终，我们找到了生存和变得明智的方法。事后我告诉他，这听起来像太阳朋克音乐，他在舞台上低声说：“那是因为它就是太阳朋克音乐。”
如果我们主要关注这一流派的文本制品-包括各种社交媒体平台上的话语，特别是它产生的类型小说-太阳朋克的更好的品质就变得无懈可击。我读了过去五年里出版的所有主要太阳朋克选集。引用“玻璃与花园：太阳朋克·萨默斯和新出炉的太阳朋克·温特斯”的编辑萨琳娜·乌利巴里的话说，“[太阳朋克]故事描绘的是适应和妥协，而不是破坏和征服…，因为她太容易被引用了。移情胜于贪婪。“。它们是解毒的解毒剂，是阴毒的解毒剂，可以抵消大阳摩托之旅造成的伤害，我是一个信徒。
然而，在阅读和欣赏了这些故事后，我注意到了一个模式。太阳朋克故事中的视角人物大致属于类似汇合点的类别；托马斯·巴德兰(Thomas Badlan)的“兰科”(The Glass and Gardens：Solarpink Winters，2020)中的乔伊尼亚(Joênia)是这一体裁中许多勇敢的科学家之一，他们争分夺秒地保护生命或帮助我们适应。年轻的制造者，如莫乃光的“蜘蛛与星星”中的生物技术修补工德尔，以及聚集在一起重新创造消失的Arcti的社区
制造者对无政府主义者有一些溢出效应。在T.X.Waston的“波士顿炉灶计划”(In Sunvault，由瓦格纳和威兰编辑的2017年选集)中，这些人在社会最左翼的流血边缘茁壮成长，为了庇护无家可归的人，他们打破了本不应该存在的规则。但许多太阳朋克角色-也是这次对话中最引人注目的-是年轻人，通常是有色人种的女性，他们正在适应严重的环境变化带来的新常态的危险，而且这样做的方式是他们的长辈没有远见或视角去做自己。在斯蒂芬妮·考克斯(索拉朋克·萨默斯饰)的“Fyrewall”中，黛莎就是这样一个人，她的职责超出了她的年龄，因为她必须修复城墙，保护未来的加州人免受肆虐的野火的伤害。所有这些故事都展示了新的或重新利用的物质资源和技术，以提高可持续性。
显然，这些声音是必要的。但是有些东西不见了。与信仰团体的交叉口被用绳子隔开了。
巴特勒的“播种者寓言”的女主人公是劳伦·奥亚·奥拉米娜(Lauren Oya Olamina)。在不可避免的混乱和转移的、商品化的痛苦降临到她的家人身上，杀死所有人，摧毁她社区剩下的东西之前，她将只是一个孩子。她会带着她的生命逃走，但她是孤儿，受到了创伤。她该怎么办？
她的故事是一个令人难以置信的强有力的故事。她非常符合太阳朋克女主人公的模样，但有一个例外：她是一位信仰领袖。为什么没有更多像她这样的角色？灰熊和桑特拉在哪里？菩萨和圣人在哪里？那些说天堂内心语言的人？劳伦·奥亚·奥莱米娜一家在哪里？
考虑到她可能代表的东西，她的缺席是可以理解的。意识形态(教条型)往往是自由思想家和第一批采用者的死亡。它产生了保守的典范，维持了残酷的等级制度，催生了令人难忘的怪物，并在其他人的历史上泼洒了漂白剂。但往往不容忽视的是，情况也恰恰相反。我想到了藏传佛教和达赖喇嘛。南方基督教领袖会议，民权时代的非暴力组织团体，其目标是救赎“美国的灵魂”，并不是历史的反常。马丁·路德·金(Martin Luther King)可能是一只山羊(对于那些无法查阅城市词典的人来说，这是有史以来最伟大的)，但如果《时代》的记忆力更强，他就不会是我们在特殊场合与之交流的唯一精神。
因此，无论是有意的还是潜意识的疏忽，这个问题都必须得到解决，才能让太阳朋克超越艺术家和进步的世俗主义者。我们需要将激进分子纳入主流。
这里有三条太阳朋克正统学说是相关的。首先，太阳朋克是一个公开包容的空间。如果它能是一个真正的男孩，反种族主义就会在它的DNA中。当我告诉你，当我告诉你，美国人口普查预测，到2045年，大多数美国人口将是有色人种(已经是18岁以下居民的情况)，我们中的大多数人都宣称有某种信仰(就在这里，巡回的Seon佛教)。
2008年，当皮尤研究中心(Pew Research)上一次提出这个问题时，95%的受访者表示相信某种更高的权力。即使在科学家中，信仰者也占大多数。如果包括世界其他地区，特别是黑色和棕色地区，这两个数字都会上升。因此，对于索拉朋克来说，在可能的未来适当地包容我们，因为对主流意识形态的厌恶和有组织宗教中经常讨论的邪恶的可能性，而将重要的指导原则黑箱包装将是错误的。邪恶无处不在。在你最喜欢的浏览器上搜索小猫，然后向下滚动大约30秒。这里有邪恶。
没有比文化更伟大、更基础的技术了。它，以及由此产生的意识形态方舟，不仅仅是来自死人的同龄人压力。文化就是软件。通常情况下，这包括一个精神平台。
“复兴之翼：太阳朋克之龙选集”(Wings of Revival：A Solarpink Dragon Colology)一书的作者克劳迪·阿尔塞奥特(Claudie Arseault)给了我们第二个信条：“(太阳朋克应该工作)来自现有的技术，来自我们已经知道是可能的东西。”这是关键。没有比文化更伟大、更基础的技术了。它，以及由此产生的意识形态方舟，不仅仅是来自死人的同龄人压力。文化就是软件。通常情况下，这包括一个精神平台。
现在，这里没有必要深入探讨意识形态哲学和抛出它们的哲学家的矛盾(不断演变)的陈述之间的精灵宝可梦竞争。只要知道他们的存在就足够了，我选择了你，J.M.巴尔金！J.M.巴尔金是耶鲁大学信息社会项目的创始人和主任，也是艾布拉姆斯言论自由研究所和骑士法律和媒体项目的主任。他的文化软件理论对意识形态采取了“矛盾的立场”，而不是贬义的立场，并认识到它带来自由和束缚的能力是平等的。但它不仅仅是一种工具。这就是工具。即使是物质技术也可以被认为是计算机/软件的延伸。你+你的Android Galaxy智能手机=Cyborg。
计算机和软件的比喻并不是要强调任何形式的身体和精神之间的笛卡尔分离。相反，它讲述了他们之间的相互依赖和主体间的互动性，社区的网络化本质，以及我们的。就像软件和硬件一样，两者缺一不可。我们不是火神。只有通过计算机软件界面的多个方面-我们赖以生存和生活在我们体内的相互关联的叙述、符号、隐喻、类别和概念联系系统-我们才能暂时地捕获和处理存在，它是巨大的，无法完全描述，不断变化，本质上是不可知的：本质上，是神圣的。作为文化的软件是表达我们的人性，满足我们的心理和生理需求，并与他人联系的唯一方式。
在穿过森林时，一位弟子问佛陀，他是否已经教授了所有关于宇宙的知识。乔达摩在捡起一把树叶后回应道：他教的关于宇宙知识的数量，与他手中与森林相关的树叶数量相当。我只教这一点：什么是解除痛苦所必需的。现在用你的意念抬起这艘宇宙飞船，你会的，我把你的背擦干。它为您提供了一些额外的经验。
把意识形态概念化成文化软件，用你的拳头来处理满满的代码。这样做会考虑到我们的内在驱动力，以及由个人产生并影响的外部力量。我们通过适应新的软件和重新安排我们目前运行的软件来改变。我们共同创造了电路板迷宫，迷宫可以引导我们理解。理解可以等同于自由度和力量。但相关的问题始终是权力属于谁？答案将我们带到非洲未来主义叙事的根源。
人们可能会得到这样的印象，对于太阳朋克故事中的许多人物来说，这是他们第一次遭遇人为的、毁灭生命的灾难。但对非洲未来主义者来说就不是这样了。
这是对非洲未来主义者的一个有效定义：我们是黑人创造性的人，他们呈现我们自己的科学未来和过去，因为白人没有，并从我们自己的批判性教育学中吸取教训。正如第一个非洲未来主义互联网社区的创始人阿隆德拉·纳尔逊(Alondra Nelson)所说，“非洲散居海外经历的升华植根于过去，但不会被它拖累。”因此，我们对灾难的盛衰循环相当熟悉，它同时存在于反乌托邦的各个维度。可用过去的概念是太阳朋克的第三个相关原则，也是它与被归类为非未来主义的作品之间的试金石。
我们的文化记忆激发了我们在压力下的韧性，它通常被编码到宗教中。回想起来，奥克塔维亚·巴特勒(Octavia Butler)看似有先见之明，与其说是先知之作，不如说是对历史的敏锐解读--1980年罗纳德·里根(Ronald Reagan)竞选总统时，罗纳德·里根(Ronald Reagan)打出了“让我们让美国再次伟大”的口号，她就是在抄袭他。历史并不完全押韵，但有人在混音中吐口水。(据“华盛顿邮报”报道，现任美国总统在2012年将这一短语注册为商标。2015年，当被告知里根以前曾使用过它时，特朗普说，“但他没有注册商标。”)。
对我们过去的类似解读将认识到美洲几乎是一个自我组织和丰富生成的过程。它隐藏在非洲裔美国人民间魔法和精神实践魔术中，隐藏在人们的视线中。根据伊冯·P·基罗(Yvonne P.Chireau)在南卡罗来纳州战前出版的“黑魔法：宗教与非裔美国人的魔术传统”(Black Magic：Religence and the African American Conjuning Tradition)一书，在南卡罗来纳州战前，一名魔术师被其他奴隶宣布为自己的主人，因为“他知道看得见的和看不见的东西。”基罗指出，亨利·哈蒙德(Henry Hammond)是奴隶制的坚定支持者，也是一名种植园主，他试图惩罚他的奴隶们的叛乱和破坏行为。但是奴隶们太相信通过魔术来保护他们：他们不顾他的惩罚继续反抗。“他们采用了哈蒙德无法接触到的概念框架。”
在巴勃罗·戈麦斯的“体验式加勒比海”中，我们发现，“加勒比黑人知识生产者欣然接受了文化铲除…的危机。创造新的知识制造形式，重申人类经验的力量，对自然做出真实的主张。“。他们是“活跃的接受者、塑造者，最重要的是，是文化适应的推动者”，创造了新的意识形态算法。
如果你知道去哪里看，历史上充斥着有色人种重新构建强加的意识形态，将它们重新连接到西方宗教符号、疏远的过去的文物和新的土著知识的综合体中的例子。升级后的软件运行多个任务：断言电源。为反叛创造道德权威。讲述美好未来的故事，结束苦难。为病毒运动组装临界质量以重新编程层级：革命。如果我们想要在接下来的事情中幸存下来，这听起来是非常有必要的。
如果你知道去哪里看，历史上充斥着有色人种重新构建强加的意识形态，将它们重新连接到西方宗教符号、疏远的过去的文物和新的土著知识的综合体中的例子。升级后的软件运行多个任务：断言电源。为反叛创造道德权威。讲述美好未来的故事，结束苦难。
太阳朋克是关于“合作”，而不是“竞争结束”。然而，历史告诉我们，通常情况下，在合作之前，一个人必须拥有创造知识的能力。在写“播种者的寓言”的过程中，巴特勒挣扎着把追求权力作为一种追求。起初，这与她截然相反(正如她在与奥克塔维亚·巴特勒(Octavia Butler)的多次采访中重申的那样，这本选集由康塞拉·弗朗西斯(Conseula Francis)编辑，2010年出版)。但她逐渐意识到，这是生存和建立新事物所必需的工具。索拉朋克最好把这一课纳入其中。
我不想给你毁了“播种者寓言”的结局。但我将给你们留下这些由劳伦·奥亚·奥拉米娜(Lauren Oya Olamina)在她的生命之书中写下的开国公理：
你触摸到的一切，你都会改变。你改变的一切都会改变你。唯一持久的真理是改变。上帝就是改变。
奥克塔维亚·巴特勒(Octavia Butler)的小说确实是一种寓言，一种支持科学的新邪教意识形态的创造故事。由于偶然的适应，索拉朋克已经演变成一个拥有与“生命之书”同义词的语言和融合的本能的社区。因此，尽管奥克塔维亚·巴特勒不认为自己是非洲未来主义者，奥拉米娜的目标是把人类带到其他星球，而不是试图拯救这个星球，但这里有一些教训，太阳朋克可以很容易地改变用途。这在某种程度上是它的事情。
想象从旧到新的方式，引发变化和被改变，更用心地与环境和其他人互动-这是Solar朋克的使命。但为了改变其他人，如果Solarpink变得更加开放，从它传统上拒绝的精神传统等意想不到的地方进行自我改造，这将有所帮助。
说转型并不像看起来那么难。它可能已经在脆弱的边缘开始了。已经有一些宗教领袖重新解释了他们的宗教传统，将围绕可持续发展的社区建设放在首位。在由Connor Carbon发起的太阳朋克项目Carbon Coast的网站上，我听说一位牧师正在进行“太阳朋克布道”。盖亚哲学正在卷土重来。甚至还有一个萌芽中的宗教团体，Terasem Faith，它的灵感来自于“播种者寓言”中的土种崇拜。这是信仰作为一种功能和科学成为福音的积极证明。
在Olamina的时间线与我们的时间线完全融合之前，需要发生变化。当希望岌岌可危时，人们不太可能在新想法上冒险。任何新事物都可能被自动归类为危险和侵入性的。但如果这是来自他们自己的叙述，太阳朋克的重新布线就不会那么可怕了。的确，这可能是对应许之地的启示。
[[这篇文章发表在“新模式”第一期上。在此购买您的副本或订阅。]]。
罗布·卡梅隆(Rob Cameron)，作家，语言学家，馅饼迷，布鲁克林的公立学校教师。当他还没有完成他非常耐心的经纪人巴里·戈德布拉特(Barry Goldblatt)的重写时，他是布鲁克林投机小说作家(Brooklyn Instrative Fiction Writers)的主要组织者，纽约科幻阅读评论系列的客座主持人和策展人，以及投机小说故事播客Kaleidocast.nyc的执行主编。
这篇文章由莉迪亚·劳伦森(Lydia Laurenson)编辑，NewMo编辑团队对其进行了轻微的事实核查。
更正：第一期这篇文章的印刷版表明，大多数美国公民是有色人种。事实上，大多数美国公民不是POC，但预计到2045年将成为POC。我们已经更正了网页版本中的句子，我们对此错误表示遗憾。

note_en:
Octavia Butler, the Black science fiction author to whom all others were compared until the coming of N.K. Jemisin, published the novel   Parable of the Sower  in 1993,  first of a trilogy. In her fictional near-future, Southern California will become but one of the newly red-lined regions earmarked for a corner pocket of Hell, because the end is nigh. I’m talking, dolphins Snapchatting us, “WE OUT! NO-THANKS-FOR-THE-MERCURY-MARINATED-FISH!”-type of climatic environmental and societal collapse. No kaiju necessary. We will die of consumption after a fifty-plus year bender, drunk on neoliberal, late-stage capitalist moonshine.
 [[This article appears in  Issue One of  The New Modality.    Buy your copy or subscribe here.]]
 Fast forward to our present non-fiction. My day job is teaching elementary school. Yet despite the allure of two months of do-whatever-I-want-time, more and more I dread the coming of summer, because glaciers the size of islands are falling into the ocean. Is this the season when my  Westworld kingdom is invaded by drought and famine? When Russia’s permafrost melts, releasing primeval microscopic disease-titans (  they are real, 2017 ) to gobble me up in a surprise Greek tragedy? When the floodgates of my Amazon-fueled desires are permanently clogged by natural man-made disaster? Of course, Prime will continue to deliver, but the markup would be understandably obscene.
  I joke because I’m afraid. Not only is this specific dystopia possible, Butler seems to have predicted it twenty-seven years ago.   Parable of the Sower  is set somewhere in the 2020s during a presidential election in which the candidate’s campaign slogan is “Make America Great Again  .”  Oh yes, and California actually   is  burning right now. This is a horrible present. I ordered the exact opposite of this nightmare. I asked for solarpunk.
  Solarpunk is the environmentally conscious speculative arts movement that best navigates the terrors ahead. Detractors label it a kind of Pollyanna utopianism full of empty calories. It’s true that the Google replicator machine will serve up a visual feast  of enforested skyscrapers and lush solar energy mushroom cities (possibly under the sea, possibly shared with amiable, buck-toothed invertebrates with ageless comedic timing). The solarpunk aesthetic will sometimes wear utopian clothing, but it’s nobody’s fault, really. Ursula K. Le Guin helped plant solarpunk seeds decades ago, in stories such as the 1972 novel   The Dispossessed,  her 1982 essay “  A Non-Euclidean View of California as a Cold Place to Be ,”  and many othe r Daoist-inspired works  .  Thus, the movement was growing long before the term was coined in 2008. So after decades of imagineering and community building, there are bound to be layers and crumbly edges. And to be fair, it is impossible to dream of Hell and not pine for its opposite.
 In truth, solarpunk is functional AF. The primary colors of its aura are red, orange, and yellow: Courageously compassionate, creatively scientific, and awakened interdependence. In many ways, it can be more rigorous than so-called “hard science fiction.” Don’t @ the messenger; I’m quoting Kim Stanley Robinson, author of   New York 2140  among many other groundbreaking stories of eco-fiction. He takes issue with the viral over-affixation of “-punk” (Google punk genres and see how many you get). However, at Boskone 57, one of the largest science fiction conventions in the country, where he was guest of honor, Robinson described a genre full of futures to defy the Anthropocene. Stories where sacrifices are made, but in the end, we find ways to survive and become wise. I told him afterwards that it sounded like solarpunk, and he stage-whispered, “That’s because it is.”
 If we focus primarily on the genre’s textual artifacts — including the discourses on the various social media platforms, but especially the genre fiction it produces — solarpunk’s better qualities become unimpeachable. I have read all the major solarpunk anthologies that have come out in the last five years. To quote Sarena Ulibarri, editor of   Glass and Gardens: Solarpunk Summers  and newly out   Solarpunk Winters,  because she’s so damn quotable, “[Solarpunk] stories depict adaptation and compromise rather than destruction and conquest… empathy over greed.” They are an antidote for the toxin, yin to counteract the damage done by the big yang motorcycle trip, and I am a believer.
 Yet after reading and enjoying these stories, I noticed a pattern. The viewpoint characters of solarpunk stories roughly fall into categories with similar confluences; Joênia from Thomas Badlan’s “Orchidae” (in   Glass and Gardens: Solarpunk Winters,  2020) is one among many intrepid scientists you will find in the genre, racing the clock to preserve life or help us adapt. Young makers, like Del the biotech tinkerer in D.K. Mok’s “The Spider and the Stars,” and the community that comes together to ceremonially re-create the vanished Arctic in Andrew Dana Hudson’s “Black Ice City” (both in   Solarpunk Summers,  2018), are also well represented in solarpunk.
 There’s some spillover from the makers into the anarchists. In T.X. Waston’s “The Boston Hearth Project” (in   Sunvault  ,  a 2017 anthology edited by Wagner and Wieland), these are people who thrive on the leftmost bleeding edge of society, breaking rules that should never have been in order to shelter the homeless. But many solarpunk characters — and most salient to this conversation — are young people, often women of color, adjusting to the dangers of the new normal brought on by severe environmental changes and doing so in ways their elders did not have the foresight or perspective to do themselves. Daesha in “Fyrewall” by Stefani Cox   (Solarpunk Summers)  is one like this, gifted with responsibilities beyond her years when she must fix the wall protecting future Californians from raging wildfires. All these stories showcase new or repurposed material resources and technologies for increased sustainability.
 Clearly these voices are necessary. But there’s something missing. The intersection with communities of faith is roped off.
  The heroine of Butler’s   Parable of the Sower  is  Lauren Oya Olamina. She will be little more than a child before inevitable mayhem and metastasized, commodified suffering rolls up on her family, murders everyone, and destroys what is left of her community. She will escape with her life, but orphaned and traumatized. What is she to do?
     Hers is an incredibly powerful story. She very much fits the mold of solarpunk heroines with one exception: She’s a faith leader. Why aren’t there more characters like her? Where are the griots and santeras? Where are the bodhisattvas and the saints? Those who speak the languages of heaven’s heart? Where are the Lauren Oya Olaminas?
 Her absence, given what she might represent, is understandable. Ideology (the dogmatic kind) has often been the death of free thinkers and first adopters. It has produced conservative paradigms that sustained brutal hierarchies, birthed unforgettable monsters, and poured bleach on other people’s history. But too often to ignore, the opposite has also been the case. Tibetan Buddhism and the Dalai Lama comes to mind. The Southern Christian Leadership Conference, the nonviolent Civil Rights Era organizing group whose goal was to redeem “the soul of America,” was not an aberration of history. Martin Luther King may have been a GOAT (Greatest Of All Time, for those without access to Urban Dictionary), but if time’s memory was more robust, his would not be the only spirit we commune with on special occasions.
 So, whether it has been a purposeful or subconscious omission, this issue must be addressed for solarpunk to move beyond artists, and progressive secularists. We need to mainstream the radical.
  There are three tenets of solarpunk orthodoxy that are relevant here. The first is that solarpunk is an overtly inclusive space. If it could be a real boy, anti-racism would be in its DNA. Keep that in mind when I tell you that the US Census  projects that by the year 2045, the majority of US population will be people of color (already the case for residents under age eighteen) and the majority of us profess some kind of faith (itinerant Seon Buddhist right here).
 In 2008, when last Pew Research asked the question, 95% of those surveyed reported belief in a higher power of some kind. Even among scientists, the believers were in the majority. Both numbers go up if you include the rest of the world, particularly the black and brown parts. So, for solarpunk to be properly inclusive of us in possible futures, it would be a mistake to blackbox significant guiding tenets because of an aversion to the dominant ideologies and the much-discussed potential for evil in organized religion. Evil is everywhere. Search for kittens on your favorite browser and scroll down for about thirty seconds. There be evil.
 There is no greater or more fundamental technology than culture. It, and the ark of ideologies that arise from it, are more than just peer pressure from dead people. Culture is   software.  And more often than not, that includes a spiritual platform.
    Claudie Arseneault, author of  Wings of Revival: A Solarpunk Dragon Anthology, gives us the second tenet: “[Solarpunk should work] from existing technologies, from things we already know are possible.” This is key. There is no greater or more fundamental technology than culture. It, and the ark of ideologies that arise from it, are more than just peer pressure from dead people. Culture is  software. And more often than not, that includes a spiritual platform.
 Now, a deep dive into the Pokémon competitions between philosophies of ideology and the contradictory (evolving) statements of the philosophers who throw them is not necessary here. It is enough to know that they exist, and that I choose you, J.M. Balkin! J.M. Balkin is the founder and director of Yale's Information Society Project, as well as director of the Abrams Institute for Freedom of Expression and the Knight Law and Media Program. His theory of cultural software takes an “ambivalent stance” on ideology, rather than a pejorative one, and recognizes its equal capacity to lead to freedom as well as bondage. But it is more than a tool. It is   the  tool. Even material technologies can be considered extensions of the computer/software. You + your Android Galaxy smartphone = Cyborg.
 The metaphor of computer and software is not meant to emphasize any kind of Cartesian separation between body and spirit. Instead, it speaks to their interdependence and intersubjectivity, the networked nature of community, of us. Like software and hardware, One is nothing without the Other. We are not Vulcans. It is only through the manifold aspects of the computer software interface — the connected narratives, symbols, metaphors, categories, and systems of conceptual associations we live by and that live in us — that we can temporally arrest and process existence, which is large beyond complete description, constantly in flux, and inherently unknowable: In essence, divine. The software that is culture is the only way to express our humanness, fulfill our psychological and physical needs, and connect with others.
 While walking through a forest, a disciple asked the Buddha if he had taught all there was to know about the universe. Gautama responded after picking up a handful of leaves: The amount he had taught, in relation to the knowledge in the universe, was comparable to the number of leaves in his hand in relation to the forest.   I teach only this: What is necessary for the relieving of suffering. Now lift this spaceship with your mind, you will, while I dry hump your back. There’s some extra XP in it for you.
 Conceptualize ideology as cultural software, processing your fist full of code. Doing so takes into account our internal drives as well as external forces produced by, and impacting, the individual. We change by adapting to new software and rearranging that which we are currently running. We co-create the circuit board maze, and the maze can lead to understanding. Understanding can equate to degrees of freedom and power. But the relevant question, then, is always power for whom? The answer brings us to the roots of Afrofuturist narratives.
  One might get the impression that for many of the characters in solarpunk stories, this has been their first run in with man-made, life obliterating disaster. Not so with Afrofuturists.
 Here’s a working definition for Afrofuturists: We are Black creatives who render our own science futures and past, because white people did not, and draw from our own critical pedagogy. As Alondra Nelson, founder of the first Afrofuturist internet community, has said, “The distillation of African diasporic experience [is] rooted in the past, but not weighed down by it.” Therefore, we are quite familiar with the boom and bust cycle of calamity, existing simultaneously in various dimensions of dystopia. The concept of the usable past is the third relevant tenet of solarpunk, and a touchstone between it and works that classify as Afrofuturist.
 Our cultural memory is what energizes resilience under stress, and it’s often coded into religion. In retrospect, Octavia Butler’s seeming prescience isn’t so much the work of an oracle but an astute reading of history — she was cribbing from Ronald Reagan’s presidential bid in 1980 after he ran with the slogan "Let's make America great again." History doesn’t exactly rhyme, but somebody’s spitting bars on the remix. (The   Washington Post   reports  that the current president of America trademarked the phrase in 2012. When told in 2015 that Reagan had used it before, Trump said, “But he didn’t trademark it.”)
 A similar reading of our past will recognize an almost self-organizing and abundantly generative process in the Americas. It’s hidden in plain sight in conjure, an African-American folk magic and spiritual practice. According to   Black Magic: Religion and the African American Conjuring Tradition  ,  by Yvonne P. Chireau, in Antebellum South Carolina, a conjure practitioner was declared by other slaves to be his own master, because “he knew things both seen and unseen.” Chireau notes that Henry Hammond, a loud proponent of slavery and a plantation owner, attempted to punish his slaves for acts of rebellion and vandalism. But the slaves’ belief in their protection via conjure was too strong: They continued to rebel despite his punishments. “They had adopted a conceptual framework to which Hammond had no access.”
 In   The Experiential Caribbean ,  by Pablo Gomez, we find, “Black Caribbean knowledge producers embraced the crisis of cultural uprooting… of the seventeenth century to create new forms of knowledge making that reasserted the power of the human experience to make truthful claims about nature.” They were, “active receptors, shapers, and most importantly, acculturating agents,” creating new ideological algorithms.
 If you know where to look, history is rife with examples of people of color reframing imposed ideologies, rewiring them into complexes of Western religious symbols, artifacts of the estranged past, and new Indigenous knowledge. The upgraded software runs multiple tasks: Asserting power. Creating moral authority for rebellion. Telling stories of better futures, an end to suffering. Assembling critical mass for viral movements to reprogram hierarchies: Revolution. This sounds awfully necessary if we’re to survive what comes next.
 If you know where to look, history is rife with examples of people of color reframing imposed ideologies, rewiring them into complexes of Western religious symbols, artifacts of the estranged past, and new Indigenous knowledge. The upgraded software runs multiple tasks: Asserting power. Creating moral authority for rebellion. Telling stories of better futures, an end to suffering.
 Solarpunk is about “collaboration with,” rather than “competition over.” However, history shows us that often, before one can collaborate, one must take power to create knowledge. Over the course of writing   Parable of the Sower,  Butler struggled with the idea of power-seeking as the quest. It was antithetical to her at first (as she reiterated over multiple interviews in   Conversations with Octavia Butler,  an anthology edited by Conseula Francis, 2010). But she came to realize that it was a tool necessary to survive and build something new. Solarpunk would do well to incorporate this lesson.
  I don’t want to ruin the end of   Parable   of the Sower  for you. But I will leave you with these founding axioms written by Lauren Oya Olamina in her Book of Life:
 All that you touch, you Change.  All that you Change, Changes you.  The only lasting truth is Change.  God Is Change.
 Octavia Butler’s novel is indeed a kind of parable, a creation tale of a new cult ideology that supports science. Solarpunk has, by accident of adaptation, evolved into a community with synonymous language to the Book of Life and syncretic, or amalgamating, instincts. So although Octavia Butler didn’t consider herself an Afrofuturist, and Olamina’s goal was to take humanity to other planets rather than try and save this one, there are lessons here that solarpunk can easily repurpose. Which is kind of its thing.
 Imagining ways to create new from old, inducing change and being changed, interacting more mindfully with the environment and other people — this is the solarpunk mission. But to transform others, it would help if solarpunk becomes more open to self-transformation from unexpected places like spiritual traditions, which it has traditionally rejected.
 Said transformation is not so hard as it may seem. It may have already begun on the crumbly edges. There are already faith leaders reinterpreting their religious traditions to prioritize community building around sustainability. On the website for   Carbon Coast,  a solarpunk project by Connor Carbon, I caught wind of a pastor giving a “solarpunk sermon.” Gaia philosophy is making a comeback. There’s even a budding religious community,   Terasem Faith , inspired by the Earthseed cult in   Parable of the Sower.  This is proof positive of faith as a function and science becoming gospel.
 Change needs to happen before Olamina’s timeline completely merges with our own. People are less likely to take risks on new ideas when hope is precarious. Anything new may be automatically categorized as dangerous and invasive. But if it’s coming from within their own narrative, a solarpunk rewiring would not be so scary. Indeed, it might be a revelation of the Promised Land.
  [[This article appears in  Issue One of The New Modality.   Buy your copy or subscribe here.]]
 Rob Cameron, author, is  a writer, linguist,  pie addict , and public school teacher in Brooklyn. When not finishing rewrites for his very patient agent, Barry Goldblatt, he is the lead organizer for the   Brooklyn Speculative Fiction Writers , a guest host and curator for the  New York Review of Science Fiction Reading Series, and managing editor of the    Kaleidocast.nyc  , a speculative fiction story podcast.
 This piece was edited by  Lydia Laurenson and lightly fact-checked by the  NewMo editorial team.
 Correction: The printed version of this article in Issue One stated that "the majority of US citizens are people of color." In fact, the majority of US citizens are not POC, but are projected to be POC by 2045. We have corrected the sentence in the web version and we regret the error.
 There's more about our transparency process at our  page about truth and transparency at  The New Modality .
title: “乐土”：宗教意识形态与太阳朋克科幻
title_en: Promised Land: Religious Ideology and Solarpunk Science Fiction

周二，缅甸终于采用了支撑电话和在线交流的通用代码，从维基百科到谷歌地图，访问一切信息都将变得容易得多。
技术专家表示，让中国与数字世界其他地区更同步的举措至关重要，但将在一个艰难的过渡期造成混乱。
缅甸是最后一个接受Unicode的国家，而且有大量的人在线。如果没有它，大多数用户将国际内容-甚至很多来自国内的内容-视为毫无意义的符号。主要网站可能无法阅读，翻译和语音识别软件也无法工作。
10月1日是“U日”，缅甸将正式采用这一新系统。从那时起，根据法律，所有来自政府办公室、电信公司、银行和媒体的电子通信都必须使用Unicode。
几年前，微软和苹果帮助其他国家实现了标准化，但西方的制裁意味着缅甸输了。
47岁的IT先驱Zaw Htut将这描述为在长达数十年的代码“战争”中赢得的最后一场战斗。
然而，转换到Unicode将是一段动荡的旅程，他警告说，最初会出现“混乱”。
战略响应团队成员Sarah Oh表示，Facebook-该国主要的社交媒体平台-已经开发了自动转换软件，为用户提供“无缝体验”。
但许多人将突然无法阅读其他应用程序上的消息，以及来自银行或电话运营商的消息，那些更换系统的人将无法再与仍在使用旧系统的朋友交流。
Unicode的本土竞争对手Zawgyi-发音为“Zaw-jee”，意思是巫师-在缅甸与世隔绝的数字生态系统中进化。
如果没有繁琐的插件，它就不能兼容，它只允许人们用缅语与其他Zawgyi用户交流，不包括该国的少数民族语言。它的笨重也意味着搜索功能不起作用，使数据存储系统瘫痪。
可能需要数年时间才能实现完全移民。Zaw Htut将突然改用Unicode与前军政府一夜之间决定缅甸司机靠右驾驶而不是靠左驾驶相比较。他说：“但我们正在以数字方式赋予国家权力。”
Joox是Spotify的地区版，该公司表示，不可能将其应用程序上的数百万首缅甸语歌曲进行转换。
随着最后期限的临近，名人和有影响力的人都在反复强调皈依的必要性，技术人员也在为蜂拥而至的询问做准备。
“有很多困惑，”23岁的计算机专业学生丁拉特·南达尔(Tin Lat Nandar)在周日的一次顺访会议上更新退休人员的手机时解释道。
仰光35岁的街头小贩米米(Mi Mi)说，她听说了这一转变，但不想改变。“我不知道该怎么做，”她说。
估计有10%到15%的手机(主要是较旧的手机)将不够先进，无法转换。
许多更便宜的非法进口设备都预装了旧系统-这让那些太穷而买不起新型号的人处于明显的劣势。
62岁的昂觉乌(Aung Kyaw Oo)笑容满面地说：“我将能够阅读缅语的维基百科(Wikipedia)。”

  Accessing everything from Wikipedia to Google Maps is about to get a lot easier in Myanmar on Tuesday when it finally adopts the universal code underpinning phone and online communication.
 Tech experts say the move to bring the country more in step with the rest of the digital world is crucial but will cause chaos in a rocky transition period.
  Myanmar is the last nation to embrace Unicode that has significant numbers of people online. Without it, most users see international content — and even a lot from within the country — as meaningless symbols. Major websites can be impossible to read, and translation and voice recognition software do not work.
 Oct. 1 is “U-Day,” when Myanmar officially will adopt the new system. From then on, all electronic communication from government offices, telecommunications companies, banks and media must be in Unicode, by law.
  Microsoft and Apple helped other countries standardize years ago, but Western sanctions meant Myanmar lost out.
 IT pioneer Zaw Htut, 47, described this as the final battle won in a decades-long code “war.”
  However, making the switch to Unicode will be a turbulent ride, and he warned that initially there will be “chaos.”
 Facebook — the main social media platform in the country — has developed automatic conversion software to give users a “seamless experience,” said strategic response team member Sarah Oh.
 But many will suddenly be unable to read messages on other apps and from banks or phone operators, and those who switch will no longer be able to communicate with friends still using the old system.
 A homegrown rival to Unicode called Zawgyi — pronounced “zaw-jee” and meaning wizard — evolved in Myanmar’s detached digital ecosystem.
   Incompatible without fiddly plug-ins, it only allows people to communicate in Burmese with other Zawgyi users, excluding the nation’s minority languages. Its clunkiness also means search functions do not work, crippling data storage systems.
 It could take years to achieve full migration. Zaw Htut compares the abrupt switch to Unicode with the former junta’s overnight decision for Myanmar drivers to drive on the right instead of the left. “But we’re digitally empowering the nation,” he said.
 Joox, a regional version of Spotify, said it is impossible to convert the millions of Burmese-language songs on its app.
 With the deadline looming, celebrities and influencers are hammering home the need to convert and technicians are preparing for a deluge of queries.
  “There’s a lot of confusion,” explained computer science student Tin Lat Nandar, 23, as she updated retirees’ phones at a drop-in session on Sunday.
 Yangon street vendor Mi Mi, 35, said she has heard about the switch but does not want to change. “I don’t know how to do it,” she said.
 An estimated 10 to 15 percent of mainly older phones will not be advanced enough to convert.
 Many cheaper, illegally imported devices come pre-installed with the old system — putting those too poor to buy newer models at a clear disadvantage.
  “I’ll be able to read Wikipedia in Burmese,” said a beaming Aung Kyaw Oo, 62, as he left a drop-in session.
title: Unicode in，Zawgyi Out：现代性终于在缅甸的数字世界迎头赶上
title_en: Unicode in, Zawgyi out: Modernity finally catches up in Myanmar's digital world

就像最近所有人一样，(马特)最近花了更多的时间在视频会议上。问题是，你自然想看屏幕，但这意味着你没有看着相机，因此，你没有进行眼神交流。如果你使用笔记本电脑，有一个相对容易的解决办法，尽管它不是特别时髦。[马特]用泡沫板做了一条黑色裹尸布，然后放进双向镜子里。这有什么用呢？好的，设置好后，你可以把一个非常薄的黑色网络摄像头对准镜子。因为裹尸布是黑色的，你可以透过镜子看到屏幕，但相机会看到你。
你在哪里能买到薄薄的黑色网络摄像头？你可以用一台旧的笔记本电脑摄像头做一个。它们很小，很容易改变用途，这是(马特)以前分享过的诀窍。作为额外的奖励，这篇帖子展示了一种简单的方法，可以拿到LED条，并制作漫射光来照亮你的网络摄像头通话。
看起来缺点是它会让键盘很难使用，所以你可能想把它和蓝牙键盘配对。它也有点笨重，但如果你在路上，看起来你可以取下镜子，折叠裹尸布，整个东西就会平放着，以便运输。
即使你不想改善你的视频聊天，这里也有一些宝贵的东西。灯光很简单，可以很好地使用一点额外的LED照明。我们可以想出很多我们想要薄型网络摄像头的理由。但让我们印象最深的是将其用作提词器的可能性。如果您的屏幕上有文本滚动(或使用鼠标滚动，您可以在直视相机的同时阅读文本)。这正是你下次竞选公职时要做的事。
我们看到的大多数双向反射镜都是用于无限远的反射镜。如果你尝试这个项目，也许可以添加一些马达和眼球跟踪，这样你就不得不进行眼神交流了。
note_en:
Like everyone lately, [Matt] has been spending more time doing video conferencing lately. The problem is you naturally want to look at the screen, but that means you aren’t looking at the camera and, thus, you aren’t making eye contact. If you use a laptop, there is a relatively easy fix, although it isn’t particularly stylish. [Matt] built a black shroud out of foam board and put in two-way mirror. How does that help? Well, with the set up, you can put a very thin black web camera pointing up towards the mirror. Because the shroud is dark, you can see the screen through the mirror, but the camera sees you.
 Where do you get a thin black web camera? You make one from an old laptop camera. They are tiny and easy to repurpose, a trick [Matt] has shared before. As a bonus, the post shows an easy way to take an LED strip and make a diffused light for lighting up your webcam call.
  It looks like the downside is it will make the keyboard difficult to use, so you might want to pair this with a Bluetooth keyboard. It is also a little bulky, but if you are on the go, it looks like you could remove the mirror, fold the shroud, and the whole thing would lay flat for transport.
 Even if you don’t want to improve your video chat, there are a few gems here. The light is simple enough and would be a good way to use a little bit of extra LED lighting. We can think of lots of reasons we want a thin web camera. But what struck us the most was the possibility of using this as a teleprompter. If you had text on your screen scrolling (or used your mouse to scroll, you could read the text while looking straight at the camera. Just the thing the next time you run for office.
 Most of the two way mirrors we see are for  infinity mirrors. If you try this project, maybe add some motors and  eye tracking so it forces you to make eye contact.
title: 双向镜像改进了视频会议
title_en: Two Way Mirror Improves Video Conferencing

伦敦-Netflix改变了获奖法国电影“Cuties”的宣传形象，此前该片被指将女孩性感。
Netflix的一位女发言人说，“我们对我们在”Cuties“中使用的不恰当的艺术作品深表歉意。”“这不好，也不能代表这部在圣丹斯获奖的法国电影。我们现在已经更新了图片和描述。“。
Netflix在流媒体服务上宣传“Cuties”的原创艺术作品中，有一张四个年轻女孩摆出挑衅性姿势的照片，一些女孩穿着热裤扭动身体。现在，宣传图片显示女主角法西亚·优素福(Fathia Youssuf)向后看。
这些抱怨--部分是由卡农阴谋论的追随者发布的--可能会让一些人感到意外，因为马伊莫纳·杜库埃的处女作“Cuties”因质疑社会和社交媒体是如何迫使年幼的孩子露骨地性行为而受到好评的。杜库埃在今年的圣丹斯电影节上被评为世界电影类最佳导演，并在今年的德国柏林电影节上获得了特别提名。
这场争吵始于周二，此前Netflix在网上发布了《Cuties》的预告片。它很快就招致了批评，电影的艺术作品也是如此。一条评论写道：“同意让女儿出演这部电影的父母都是皮条客。”
一些人仍在呼吁将这部定于9月9日在Netflix上映的电影从流媒体服务中删除。周五已有超过17万人签名的一份在线请愿书敦促Netflix不要发布这份请愿书。周四发布的请愿书的最新版本写道：“问题不在于艺术品，而在于11岁的孩子为了成人观看乐趣而扭动身体的事实。”“把它拿下来，我们有截图，”它补充道。
要求撤下这部电影的呼声在QAnon阴谋论的支持者中获得了特别的支持，他们认为民主党高层和艺术家玛丽娜·阿布拉莫维奇(Marina Abramoc)等名人是一个全球贩卖儿童团伙的幕后黑手。“Netflix只是将恋童癖正常化了，”一个支持QAnon的Facebook页面在周五发帖称。
对艺术品的改变只是Netflix出于道德原因面临从其流媒体平台上删除内容的压力的最新例子。今年7月，该公司取消了在土耳其的一部系列剧的制作，此前当局因该剧中有一名同性恋角色而拒绝了它的许可。2018年，Netflix顺应沙特阿拉伯政府的要求，从该国的平台上删除了节目《与哈桑·明哈吉的爱国者法案》(Patriot Act With Hasan Minhaj)的一集，因为其中包括对王储穆罕默德·本·萨勒曼(Mohammed Bin Salman)的批评。
这部电影本身-在法国以“米格诺内斯”的名字发行-并没有引起任何这样的争吵。杜库雷的处女作，讲述了11岁的艾米(优素福饰)努力寻找自己生活中的位置的故事。在家里，艾米不得不取悦她的母亲，一位来自塞内加尔的穆斯林，但她被一群有自己的舞蹈团的朋友所吸引。
今年6月，杜库埃在圣丹斯电影节(Sundance Film Festival)接受采访时说，她在巴黎参加了一场活动，观看了一群11岁的孩子表演一种高度性感的舞蹈，她在一定程度上萌生了“Cuties”的想法。“我太震惊了，”她说。“对我来说，那只是，‘哦，我的上帝。我看到的是什么？‘“。她补充说，许多孩子的父母也在观看节目，他们穿着传统的宗教服装，文化冲击让她着迷。
她说，在研究这部电影的过程中，杜库埃开始担心社交媒体是如何迫使孩子们穿得挑衅性的。
该片于8月19日在法国影院上映，《Cuties》并未引发任何争议。克拉丽斯·法布雷(Clarisse Fabre)为“世界报”(Le Monde)评论这部电影时说：“电影制作人巧妙地克制住了对舞蹈套路中非常露骨的性别化的评判。”她把它命名为“必看之地”。另一家报纸“费加罗报”(Le Figaro)也称其为“值得一看的电影”。
圣丹斯的影评人也不认为这部电影是恋物癖。菲奥努拉·哈利根在“银幕日报”上写道：“青春期前身体扭曲的场景显然是为了震撼成熟的观众，让他们反思当今纯真的毁灭。”但她补充说，这部电影最终失败了，因为它太过努力地试图挑起谴责。
杜库埃没有对这场大惊小怪或艺术品发表评论，她的代表也没有回应周五的采访请求。但她可能会欣赏一些正在进行的关于社会上儿童性别化的辩论。“我们不能继续对此视而不见，”她在圣丹斯接受采访时说。
note_en:
LONDON — Netflix has changed the promotional image for “Cuties,” an award-winning French film, after it was accused of sexualizing girls.
 “We’re deeply sorry for the inappropriate artwork that we used for ‘Cuties,’” a spokeswoman for Netflix said. “It was not OK, nor was it representative of this French film which won an award at Sundance. We’ve now updated the pictures and description.”
 Netflix’s  original artwork promoting “Cuties” on the streaming service featured a picture of four young girls posed provocatively, some twerking while wearing hot pants. Now, the promotional image shows the lead actress, Fathia Youssouf, looking over her shoulder.
 The complaints — posted in part by followers of the  QAnon conspiracy theory — may have come as a surprise to some, given that “Cuties,” the debut feature by Maïmouna Doucouré, has been praised for questioning how society and social media pressure young children into acting in sexually explicit ways. Doucouré was named best director in the world cinema category at this year’s Sundance Film Festival and  received a special mention at this year’s Berlinale festival in Germany.
 The row began on Tuesday after Netflix published the  trailer for “Cuties” online. It quickly drew criticism, as did the film’s artwork. “Parents who agreed to put their girls in this movie are pimps,” read one comment.
  Some are still calling for the film, scheduled to land on Netflix on Sep. 9, to be deleted from the streaming service. One online  petition, which had over 170,000 signatures on Friday, urged Netflix not to release it. “THIS PROBLEM ISNT THE ARTWORK ITS THE FACT ITS ABOUT TWERKING ELEVEN YEAR OLDS FOR ADULT VIEWING PLEASURE,” read an update to the petition, posted on Thursday. “TAKE IT DOWN WE HAVE THE SCREENSHOTS,” it added.
 Calls to remove the film have gained particular traction among supporters of the  QAnon conspiracy theory, who believe top Democrats and celebrities like  the artist Marina Abramovic are behind a global child trafficking ring. “Netflix just normalized pedophilia,” one  QAnon-supporting Facebook page posted on Friday.
 The change to the artwork is only the latest example of  Netflix facing pressure to remove content from its streaming platform on moral grounds. In July, it  canceled production of a series in Turkey after authorities refused it a permit because the show included a gay character. In 2018, Netflix complied with a request from the Saudi Arabian government and removed an episode of the show “Patriot Act With Hasan Minhaj” from the platform in that country because it included criticism of Crown Prince Mohammed bin Salman.
 The film itself — released as “Mignonnes” in France — has not caused any such rows. Doucouré’s debut feature, it follows the story of 11-year-old Amy (Youssouf) as she struggles to find her place in life. At home, Amy has to please her mother, a Muslim from Senegal, but she’s drawn to a group of friends who have their own dance troupe.
 Doucouré  said in June during an interview at the Sundance Film Festival that the idea for “Cuties” partly came to her after she attended an event in Paris, where she watched a group of 11-year-olds performing a highly sexualized dance. “I was so shocked,” she said. “For me, it was just, ‘Oh my God. What am I seeing?’” Many of the children’s parents, who were also watching the show, wore traditional religious dress, she added, and the culture shock fascinated her.
 While researching the film, Doucouré became concerned about how social media pressures children to dress provocatively, she said.
 In France, where the film was released in theaters on Aug. 19, “Cuties” has not stirred any controversy. Clarisse Fabre, reviewing the film for Le Monde,  said, “The filmmaker skillfully refrains from passing judgment on the very explicit sexualization of the dance routines.” She named it a “must see.” Le Figaro, another newspaper, also labeled it a “ film to see.”
 Reviewers at Sundance didn’t see the film as fetishistic either. “The sight of twerking preteen bodies is explicitly designed to shock mature audiences into a contemplation of today’s destruction of innocence,”  wrote Fionnuala Halligan in Screen Daily, but she added that the film ultimately failed because it tried too hard to provoke censure.
 Doucouré hasn’t commented on the fuss or the artwork, and her representative did not respond to an interview request on Friday. But she may appreciate some of the ongoing debate about the sexualization of children in society. “We can’t continue to close our eyes about that,” she said in the interview at Sundance.
title: Netflix被指将女孩性感，撤下“Cuties”的艺术作品
title_en: Netflix, Accused of Sexualizing Girls, Pulls Artwork for ‘Cuties’
